main_summary.summary: Gertrude Newsome Jackson (b. 1923, Madison, Illinois) recounts her work as a community organizer in Marvell, Arkansas (Phillips County) during the 1960s civil rights movement. After her family relocated to an 88-acre cotton farm in Arkansas following her grandfather's death, Jackson attended a one-room segregated school while white students rode buses to a well-equipped facility. Her organizing began when a newly built Black school suffered repeated sewage backups that the Marvell school board refused to address; her husband (called 'Hog' Jackson) led the community in pulling children out of school, the area's first boycott. SNCC field workers Howard Himmelbaum and Myrtle Glascoe lived with relatives and held weekly meetings at Jones Ridge that drew packed churches. Jackson and her husband endured a cross burning in their yard, an arson attack on their farm shop, sugar in their cotton picker's fuel tank, and a roadside shooting incident. The Jacksons served as named plaintiffs in the federal school-desegregation suit decided by Judge Owen Harris with attorney John Walker, which produced a single-bus integration order. Jackson later helped found the Boys Girls Adults Community Development Center (BGACDC) with Gale Thrower, building Head Start home-visit programs, housing units, and youth tutoring. Reverend Jerome Turner and grandson Theodore Eldridge Chilling provide supplementary testimony.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Jackson's testimony documents civil rights organizing in the rural Mississippi Delta region of eastern Arkansas (Phillips County, Marvell), a geography less represented in mainstream movement narratives than urban Alabama or Mississippi. Her account specifies SNCC's Arkansas Project field workers Howard Himmelbaum and Myrtle Glascoe by name, the local Black leadership network including Reverend Overtis Adams and John Hamilton, and the federal litigation chain (Judge Owen Harris, attorney John Walker) that produced the Marvell school-integration order.

The interview also captures the post-movement continuity of organizing work through the founding of BGACDC, providing a primary-source account of how Head Start partnerships, housing development through FHA, and community-center infrastructure were built by movement veterans in the 1970s and beyond. Supplementary testimony from Reverend Jerome Turner and grandson Theodore Eldridge documents intergenerational transmission of movement memory.

chapters.part: From Illinois to an Arkansas Farm
chapters.title: Back to the 88-Acre Farm
chapters.summary: Interviewed in Marvell, Arkansas, Gertrude Jackson says she was born in Madison, Illinois in 1923, and that after her grandfather died her father moved the family back to Arkansas to take over the family's 88-acre cotton farm for his mother. She names her six brothers and four sisters and recalls the shock of a country childhood after Illinois.

chapters.part: From Illinois to an Arkansas Farm
chapters.title: Everybody in the Same Room
chapters.summary: Jackson contrasts her short walk to school in Illinois with the four or five miles she walked in Arkansas to a single room holding first through eighth grade around a central heater, about sixty children with nails on the back wall for their coats, tracing her own schooling to tenth grade in Marvell, a later GED, and college courses through Mid-Delta. She remembers the white schools beautiful and whole while Black children walked as the bus passed, and a white boy chanting a slur from the bus window until a boy waited with a stick and popped his hand.

chapters.part: From Illinois to an Arkansas Farm
chapters.title: So We Can Have Some Swings and Seesaws
chapters.topic: A Daughter Wishes She Were White
chapters.summary: Jackson tells, then retells for the camera, how her daughter standing in the kitchen said she wished she were white; pressed gently for the reason, the girl explained it was so she could have swings, seesaws, and the pretty white school, while their own school had only the naked ground. Jackson says at the time she let it pass because she knew that was just the way it was.

chapters.part: The Sewage and the First Boycott
chapters.title: The Bathroom Flooded Today
chapters.summary: Jackson explains what first moved her to organize: the Marvell district built a school joining about six rural communities, but after a few years its sewage began backing up, flooding the bathrooms until the boys had to clean it with boxes from the store. When repeated trips to the school board brought only the retort that the children did not know how to use the bathroom, her husband resolved to act.

chapters.part: The Sewage and the First Boycott
chapters.title: If I Could Get These People to Take the Children Out
chapters.summary: Jackson recounts her husband, whom people called Hog, going through the community and reaching parents through the men at their country store to ask that the children be kept home, so that only eight or ten pupils showed up and the school sat near empty for most of the term. She describes how the superintendent's brother first declared the plumbing fine until Howard called the state sanitation office from Little Rock, which made them dig it up and redo it so the children could return.

chapters.part: The Movement Comes to Jones Ridge
chapters.title: It's Somebody Here From the Civil Rights
chapters.summary: Jackson describes how civil rights workers came and met at neighbor Overtis Adams's house, prompting four or five people from her Jones Ridge community to gather and listen. She names the SNCC organizers Myrtle Glascoe and Howard Himmelbaum, who filled a church every Thursday night but pushed the local people to lead themselves rather than always being out front.

chapters.part: The Movement Comes to Jones Ridge
chapters.title: Like Another One of My Children
chapters.summary: Jackson recalls Howard Himmelbaum first living with her uncle until the uncle grew nervous about a white man being seen leaving his house by the store, then moving to live with her cousin Carrie Davis, and Myrtle Glascoe being like another daughter among her children. She says the organizers urged people to stand up and be bold, but many came only to listen out of fear, teachers were kept from meetings to protect their jobs, and one man told her husband Jack he was for the cause but too scared to join.

chapters.part: Crosses, Guns, and the County Fair
chapters.title: There She Is, There She Is
chapters.summary: Jackson recalls a woman in the drugstore pointing her out to a white man as the one doing civil rights work, and remembers crosses burned at the homes of Reverend Masco Anderson and the leader John Hamilton. She describes how, hearing a gang planned to attack Hamilton's remote house, ten or twelve men met there with guns and stood watch all night.

chapters.part: Crosses, Guns, and the County Fair
chapters.title: The News Is Good Tonight
chapters.summary: Jackson says the local civil rights activity never made the news, but recalls how the family hushed the children whenever the television showed the Little Rock school integration, and how her excited little girl once announced that the news was good that night. She describes telling her children that, as in Marvell, whites and Blacks went to separate schools and troops came when Little Rock tried to integrate.
related_events: Little Rock Central High School integration

chapters.part: Crosses, Guns, and the County Fair
chapters.title: Let's Boycott That Fair
chapters.summary: Jackson recounts deciding at a boycott meeting, on Reverend Anderson's suggestion, to also boycott the county fair while the children were out of school. She describes urging a Pine Bluff college choir not to perform, parking by the empty fairgrounds with another woman, white and Black boys running off after hearing they were not supposed to attend, and the fair making no money that year.

chapters.part: They Knew I Wasn't Going to Stop
chapters.title: The Shop's on Fire
chapters.topic: Crosses, a Burned Truck, and Sabotage
chapters.summary: Jackson tells how a cross was burned in the shape into the grass of her own yard, and how one night her daughter saw the equipment shop on fire; her husband, expecting an attack, ran out without his shoes and dragged the burning truck by tractor across the field, the fire set by a rag stuffed in the gas tank. She adds that someone locked up a cotton picker in a far field, believed done by putting sugar in the tank, though her brother-in-law James repaired it and they never learned who did it.

chapters.part: They Knew I Wasn't Going to Stop
chapters.title: He Shot Across the Hood of My Car
chapters.topic: Shots Fired on the Road Home
chapters.summary: Jackson describes a truck slowing beside her as she drove home from work, then speeding past while the passenger fired a pistol across the front of her car; she kept it from her husband for a month so he would not put himself in danger searching for the men. She recalls him later leaving for a contested farm road with his pistol, saying they might get him but he could get some of them too.

chapters.part: Something for the Children
chapters.title: Just Something for Children
chapters.topic: The Idea for a Community Center
chapters.summary: Jackson says the idea for the center came to her at her Mid-Delta desk as a persistent inner urging of just something for children, until Gale Thrower, the agriculture teacher, offered to fix up an unused room. She describes her aim that every child see a book before starting school, sending retired teachers like Maxine Hathaway and Anna May Smith into homes to read, and arranging nurses from Helena to give the children their shots.

chapters.part: Something for the Children
chapters.title: Forty Miles to Head Start
chapters.summary: Jackson recounts how, once BGACDC was founded and a building added, Helena staff brought every service to the center, and describes her first Mid-Delta job transporting children to Head Start, driving roughly forty miles each morning and evening across the fields. She tells how the agency sought her out as the only person in the district with a station wagon, which her husband at first refused for her.

chapters.part: Something for the Children
chapters.title: The Twin Who Knocked the Work Off the Wall
chapters.summary: Jackson remembers a disruptive twin boy in Head Start who would circle the group hitting children and knocking work off the wall, until home visits revealed he was not coming from a comfortable home; she would calm him with walks and field trips and kept him the two years a four-year-old could attend.

chapters.part: Something for the Children
chapters.title: Stay Off That Man's Place
chapters.summary: Jackson tells of a family living on a white man's place whose boss ordered them to keep the woman driving the white station wagon away because he wanted no one connected to Martin Luther King on his land; within a year the family had built their own house. She reflects that the move came just in time, since by then her home-start work would have brought ten families together for monthly meetings.

chapters.part: Something for the Children
chapters.title: Stand Up for Yourself
chapters.summary: Jackson says her father, a farmer, left Arkansas for a job at a factory called Commonwealth before returning, then answers what she most wants her descendants to know of the movement work: to stand up for themselves, get involved, and attend PTA and school meetings, a charge she repeats after noise interrupts the take.

chapters.part: The Court Case and the Yellow Bus
chapters.title: Trucks Bumper to Bumper
chapters.topic: The Armed Standoff at the School
chapters.summary: Jackson explains that when the district's grade-by-grade integration reached year two the white community balked, so when Myrtle proposed simply walking the children over without the choice forms, word leaked and the school block was ringed with trucks bumper to bumper, two or three armed men on each. She and her cousin drove around to see it, while the boycotting children read books at her church.

chapters.part: The Court Case and the Yellow Bus
chapters.title: We Wanted to Go to Court
chapters.topic: A Plaintiff, a Judge, and the Yellow Bus
chapters.summary: Jackson recounts how Mr. Himmelbaum said they needed someone willing to be the plaintiff, and her husband volunteered because no one else would, leading to a court case where, after a couple of days, Judge Owen Harris ordered integration and an end to separate white and Black buses on the same route, with young lawyer John Walker correcting white witnesses who used a slur. She closes the story, told twice for the camera, of her son answering the family's habitual question of whether the coming bus was white or black with the word yellow.

chapters.part: Reverend Turner and BGACDC Today
chapters.title: Four Components in Poplar Grove
chapters.summary: Reverend Jerome Turner, youth and family director, describes BGACDC near Poplar Grove and Marvell in Phillips County, laying out its four components: youth and family services, economic development, housing, and administration. He details the BGACDC Manor low-income housing, the after-school Delta Reach and Teach program co-run with the Marvell-Elaine district, the Best Food in Town cafeteria with summer jobs, and the USDA- and FDA-partnered Delta Obesity Research Project that found residents deficient in vitamin D despite ample sunlight.

chapters.part: Reverend Turner and BGACDC Today
chapters.title: We Call Her Our Ambassador
chapters.topic: Mrs. Jackson's Legacy at the Center
chapters.summary: Turner introduces Gertrude Jackson as the center's ambassador, in her eighties and present since the late 1970s, who founded it with Gale Thrower and other community members so boys, girls, and adults alike would have a place, giving it the name Boys Girls Adults Community Development Center. He says hundreds of young people, among them returning investment consultant Marcus Thrower, came through its mentoring programs to become entrepreneurs, lawyers, doctors, and educators.

chapters.part: Reverend Turner and BGACDC Today
chapters.title: God Just Put Him in My Eyesight
chapters.summary: Turner tells how Mrs. Jackson, having seen him speak at a meeting years earlier, sought him out to work at BGACDC and praises her willingness to give everything and to see the good in people. Jackson adds her own account of spotting the young man at a meeting, knowing he would be great, and waiting fifteen years for him to grow into the role.

chapters.part: Reverend Turner and BGACDC Today
chapters.title: On the Highway Where People Can See It
chapters.summary: Jackson describes how executive director Beatrice wanted housing, so they worked with the FHA to build it over white opposition that claimed a housing project would bring drugs, and how, after the first site fell through, they insisted on a highway location rather than a hidden country road. She says the project holds about thirty-nine units, with twenty-some more across town.

chapters.part: A Grandson's Inheritance
chapters.title: It Was Just Another Day
chapters.summary: Theodore Eldridge, called TJ, introduces himself as Jackson's grandson through her oldest daughter Juanita and a USDA farm service agent who oversees her land, recalling driving tractors from age eleven. He describes his grandparents as great storytellers who recounted hardships without bitterness or a victim's framing, telling it simply as what happened and what they did to get over it.

chapters.part: A Grandson's Inheritance
chapters.title: Oh Yeah, We Know Her
chapters.topic: The Center as the Community's Place
chapters.summary: TJ reflects that his grandmother's nonchalant idea to help children grew into a center affecting hundreds of families, so that from the police and mayor to state representatives everyone in the Marvell area knows the Jackson family. He and Jackson describe the center as the community's only gathering place, packed for every holiday with decorations the women prepared, and note that after the highway took their parking there is nowhere else to meet, the center standing as an example of when a no-longer-tight community came together to do something for themselves.

chapters.part: A Grandson's Inheritance
chapters.title: Kind of Like a Fairytale
chapters.summary: Asked by her grandson whether she enjoyed the movement work, Jackson says she really did, finding the word not fear but being out there in action and never fearing for a friend. TJ reflects that he never heard a story of fear, that it felt almost an afterthought, like a fairytale, even as Martin Luther King and others were dying for the same things.

chapters.part: A Grandson's Inheritance
chapters.title: Several Other Communities Were Influenced
chapters.summary: Reverend Turner closes by naming centers influenced by BGACDC, including the Lee County Community Development Center in Marianna and efforts in Elaine and across an Arkansas, Mississippi, and Louisiana collaborative under the Foundation of Midsouth, crediting the travels of Mrs. Jackson and the executive director. Jackson remarks that this is what the Lord showed her in the young man years before.

loc_video.caption: Gertrude Newsome Jackson oral history interview conducted by LaFleur Paysour in Marvell, Arkansas, 2010 November 22
